flashcards.q: Define: Consumer
flashcards.a: Any person who purchases goods or services for personal use or consumption, not for commercial purposes or resale.

flashcards.q: Why do consumers need legal protection?
flashcards.a: Due to information asymmetry and power imbalance with sellers. Consumers lack information and bargaining power; sellers are organized and control supply.

flashcards.q: What is exploitation in the marketplace?
flashcards.a: When sellers take unfair advantage of consumers through unfair weights, hidden charges, adulteration, defective goods, false advertising, or market manipulation.

flashcards.q: Define: Adulteration
flashcards.a: Mixing inferior materials with genuine products to reduce costs while charging full price. Example: adding water to milk.

flashcards.q: What does ISI mark certify?
flashcards.a: ISI (Indian Standards Institution) mark certifies that products meet national quality and safety standards.

flashcards.q: What does Agmark certify?
flashcards.a: Agmark certifies edible oils, cereals, and spices are of good quality and free from adulterants.

flashcards.q: What does Hallmark certify?
flashcards.a: Hallmark certifies gold jewellery meets purity and fineness standards.

flashcards.q: In which year was the Consumer Protection Act enacted in India?
flashcards.a: 1986. Known as COPRA (Consumer Protection Act 1986).

flashcards.q: What is COPRA?
flashcards.a: Central legislation protecting consumer rights, establishing consumer rights framework, creating grievance redressal systems, and defining business responsibilities.

flashcards.q: Name the six consumer rights guaranteed by COPRA.
flashcards.a: Right to safety, right to information, right to choice, right to seek redressal, right to consumer education, and right to representation.

flashcards.q: Define: Right to Safety
flashcards.a: Consumer right to be protected against hazardous goods and services that could damage health or property.

flashcards.q: Define: Right to Information
flashcards.a: Consumer right to know product details—ingredients, price, expiry date, batch number, manufacturer address—enabling informed choice.

flashcards.q: Define: Right to Choice
flashcards.a: Consumer right to select among available products and refuse unwanted goods without coercion or forced bundling.

flashcards.q: Define: Right to Seek Redressal
flashcards.a: Consumer right to access grievance mechanisms when wronged and seek compensation for damage or loss.

flashcards.q: Define: Right to Consumer Education
flashcards.a: Consumer right to access information enabling informed purchasing decisions and awareness of exploitation methods.

flashcards.q: Define: Right to Representation
flashcards.a: Consumer right to have voice in government decisions affecting consumers through consumer organizations.

flashcards.q: What is the Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (CDRC)?
flashcards.a: Three-tier judicial system (District, State, National) established under COPRA to resolve consumer complaints and disputes.

flashcards.q: What cases does District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission handle?
flashcards.a: Claims up to Rs 1 crore. Handles local consumer complaints about retailers and service providers.

flashcards.q: What is the jurisdiction of State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission?
flashcards.a: Cases involving claims between Rs 1 crore and Rs 10 crore. Hears appeals from District Commissions.

flashcards.q: What jurisdiction does National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission have?
flashcards.a: Cases involving claims exceeding Rs 10 crore. Final appellate authority ensuring uniform consumer protection across states.

flashcards.q: What information must be displayed on packaged goods?
flashcards.a: Ingredients, price, batch number, manufacture date, expiry date, and manufacturer's address.

flashcards.q: What does MRP stand for and what does it indicate?
flashcards.a: MRP stands for Maximum Retail Price. It is the highest price at which a product can be sold; consumers can bargain for lower prices.

flashcards.q: What is the Right to Information (RTI) Act?
flashcards.a: Law enacted by Government of India (2005) ensuring citizens access to information about government department functions.

flashcards.q: Define: Unfair Trade Practice
flashcards.a: Deceptive or exploitative business practices including false advertising, undisclosed charges, shortchanging, and manipulation.

flashcards.q: What is the Consumer Movement?
flashcards.a: Organized effort by consumers and organizations to protect rights, challenge exploitation, and advocate for fair markets and consumer protection laws.

flashcards.q: When is National Consumers' Day observed in India?
flashcards.a: December 24, commemorating the enactment of Consumer Protection Act 1986.

flashcards.q: What was the UN Guidelines for Consumer Protection?
flashcards.a: Adopted in 1985, it provided international framework for countries to adopt consumer protection measures.

flashcards.q: How many consumer organizations operate in India?
flashcards.a: Over 2,000 consumer organizations, though only 50-60 are well-organized and recognized for their work.

flashcards.q: What is CUTS?
flashcards.a: Consumer Unity and Trust Society, a consumer organization working in India for 40+ years, advocating for consumer awareness and protection.

flashcards.q: What is Consumers International?
flashcards.a: Umbrella body of global consumer organizations with over 200 member organizations from 100+ countries.

flashcards.q: What makes a market 'unfair' according to the chapter?
flashcards.a: When few powerful producers face scattered small-quantity consumers. Large companies manipulate markets through false information and market control.

flashcards.q: Define: Quality Certification
flashcards.a: Official mark or certification indicating that products meet government-approved safety and quality standards.

flashcards.q: For which products is quality certification mandatory?
flashcards.a: Health-affecting products: LPG cylinders, food colours, packaged drinking water, cement. Mandatory to ensure public safety.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between certification being 'mandatory' and 'voluntary'?
flashcards.a: Mandatory: all producers must get certified or face penalties. Voluntary: producers choose to certify; uncertified products may still be sold.

flashcards.q: What does it mean that 'certification is not compulsory for all producers'?
flashcards.a: For most products, certification is voluntary. Producers can choose whether to get certified, though certification builds consumer trust.

flashcards.q: Define: Defective Goods
flashcards.a: Products with manufacturing faults or expired dates that fail to perform their intended function properly.

flashcards.q: How do defective goods differ from adulterated goods?
flashcards.a: Defective: have faults or expired; fail to work. Adulterated: intentionally mixed with inferior materials; deceptive quality.

flashcards.q: What information is required on medicine packets?
flashcards.a: Directions for proper use, side effects, risks associated with usage, and expiry date.

flashcards.q: What information is required on garments?
flashcards.a: 'Instructions for washing'—explaining proper care and maintenance to preserve product quality.

flashcards.q: If a product is defective and within expiry period, what can a consumer do?
flashcards.a: Ask for replacement. If expiry wasn't printed, manufacturer cannot shift responsibility to shopkeeper.

flashcards.q: What is the purpose of keeping bills and receipts?
flashcards.a: Documentation enabling complaint filing if product is defective or rights violated. Without receipts, evidence is difficult to establish.

flashcards.q: Define: Consumer Organization
flashcards.a: Voluntary non-governmental organization working to protect consumer rights through awareness, representation, and advocacy.

flashcards.q: What are the main activities of consumer organizations?
flashcards.a: Education about rights, helping file complaints, representing consumers in disputes, monitoring markets, and advocating policy changes.

flashcards.q: What is the role of consumer organizations in market monitoring?
flashcards.a: Check shops for unfair weights, verify prices, test products for adulteration, and identify deceptive practices.

flashcards.q: Why can't individual consumers effectively challenge large companies alone?
flashcards.a: Power imbalance: large companies are wealthy and organized; individual consumers are scattered and lack resources. Collective action through organizations is more effective.

flashcards.q: What case illustrates the right to safety?
flashcards.a: Reji Mathew's case: improper anaesthesia during tonsillectomy caused brain damage. Consumer court held hospital responsible for negligence.

flashcards.q: What case illustrates the right to choice?
flashcards.a: Abirami's case: she paid for two-year coaching course but opted out after one year. Court ordered refund, upholding right to choose.

flashcards.q: What case illustrates the right to seek redressal?
flashcards.a: Prakash's case: money order didn't reach on time. He filed complaint in District Commission, which awarded compensation.

flashcards.q: What is the significance of Abirami's case for educational institutions?
flashcards.a: Institutions cannot charge lump sum for entire duration in advance; must allow proportional refunds; cannot deny right to choose.

flashcards.q: What penalties did court impose in Abirami's case?
flashcards.a: Institute ordered to refund Rs 28,000, fined Rs 25,000 for frivolous appeal, and pay Rs 7,000 compensation and litigation costs.

flashcards.q: What steps did Prakash take to file his consumer complaint?
flashcards.a: Contacted local consumer protection council for guidance, filled registration form, and filed complaint in District Commission.

flashcards.q: What is the typical process in Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission?
flashcards.a: Register complaint, commission sends notice to defendant, hearing held, evidence examined, written order passed with decision and compensation.

flashcards.q: Can consumers file cases without lawyers in consumer courts?
flashcards.a: Yes. COPRA enables consumers to represent themselves without lawyers, making justice accessible to poor and vulnerable.

flashcards.q: What is a 'class action suit'?
flashcards.a: Consumer group suing collectively on behalf of multiple consumers with similar grievances, amplifying individual voices.

flashcards.q: How have consumer courts adapted to modern times?
flashcards.a: Now accept online complaints and conduct cases through video conferencing, reducing travel burden and access barriers.

flashcards.q: What challenges does the consumer redressal process face?
flashcards.a: Becoming cumbersome and expensive; takes 1-2 years; requires documentation; many consumers cannot afford the process despite free court services.

flashcards.q: Why is consumer awareness particularly important for vulnerable populations?
flashcards.a: Poor, rural, and vulnerable groups are most exploited but least aware of rights and remedies. Awareness enables protection.

flashcards.q: What are the practices of a well-informed consumer?
flashcards.a: Reading labels, checking expiry dates, verifying quality marks, comparing prices, keeping receipts, understanding agreements, and knowing where to complain.

flashcards.q: How does a well-informed consumer contribute to fair markets?
flashcards.a: Informed consumers verify products, compare prices, and demand fair treatment. This disciplines businesses and forces fair practices.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between consumer rights and market fairness?
flashcards.a: Consumer rights address market failures caused by information asymmetry and power imbalance, enabling fair transactions and informed choice.

flashcards.q: What was amended in COPRA in 2019?
flashcards.a: Expanded to include online purchases and digital services; made service providers and manufacturers liable for defects.

flashcards.q: What is mediation in consumer disputes?
flashcards.a: Settlement of disputes with help of neutral intermediary outside Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, now included as option.

flashcards.q: What government ministry oversees consumer affairs in India?
flashcards.a: Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution—responsible for consumer policy and protection.
